ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general green tea beverage class (FOODON_03301103). No specific jasmine green tea with chia seed entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code AN0GJ covers green tea as a beverage category. No specific jasmine-scented green tea with chia seed entry exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 covers green tea as a substance. No specific jasmine green tea with chia seed concept exists; closest_parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 ml (300 g), representing a typical café cup of jasmine green tea with chia seeds as served in Singapore cafés.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, green, brewed (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #14355) and Chia seeds (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #12006); composite estimate for jasmine green tea with chia seed at typical café dilution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Jasmine green tea with chia seed is not a single entry in any primary regional database. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed green tea (per 100 g) and chia seeds, weighted to reflect a typical café preparation: approximately 250 ml brewed jasmine green tea plus 10 g dry chia seeds per 300 ml serving. Per-100 g values are derived from this composite. Jasmine scenting contributes negligible macronutrients. Chia seed contribution is the dominant macro source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of green tea and chia seed beverages; analogous estimate based on green tea (GI ~1, negligible carbohydrate) and chia seeds (GI ~1, low available carbohydrate) per University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for jasmine green tea with chia seed. Green tea is a near-zero glycemic beverage; chia seeds have a very low GI (approximately 1) due to high fibre and low available carbohydrate content. GI set to 1 (lowest valid positive value) per convention for essentially zero-GI beverages. GL per 300 g serving = (1 × 5.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.1. University of Sydney GI Database consulted for chia seed and green tea analogues.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~3 mg per 100 g) and presence of potassium from chia seeds
hypertension.guidance: Jasmine green tea with chia seed is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its very low sodium content (~9 mg per 300 ml serving) and modest potassium contribution from chia seeds, which may support healthy blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ~1), low available carbohydrate, and high dietary fibre from chia seeds
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it has a negligible glycemic index and glycemic load; the soluble fibre from chia seeds may further attenuate postprandial glucose response, though added sweeteners should be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (ALA) and dietary fibre from chia seeds; antioxidant catechins from green tea
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Jasmine green tea with chia seed provides alpha-linolenic acid (ALA) and soluble fibre from chia seeds alongside antioxidant catechins from green tea, which are associated with reduced LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jasmine green tea with organic chia seed is a lightly caffeinated beverage served in Singapore cafés, consisting of jasmine-scented green tea brewed from Camellia sinensis leaves and hydrated organic chia seeds (Salvia hispanica). The drink is typically served cold or at room temperature in a cup of approximately 300 ml. The jasmine green tea base contributes a floral, mildly astringent flavour with subtle vegetal notes characteristic of green tea, while the chia seeds swell in the liquid to form a soft, tapioca-like gel texture that adds body and a mild nutty undertone. Nutritionally, the beverage is very low in calories (approximately 16 kcal per 100 g) and carbohydrates, making it a low-glycemic-index drink suitable for weight management and blood sugar control. Chia seeds are a notable source of dietary fibre (including soluble mucilaginous fibre), omega-3 fatty acids (ALA), calcium, magnesium, phosphorus, and zinc. Green tea contributes polyphenolic antioxidants, particularly epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) and other catechins, which are associated with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective effects. The beverage is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its low glycemic load, low sodium, and beneficial fatty acid and fibre profile. In Singapore, this drink is commonly found in café menus alongside other tea-based beverages and is popular as a health-conscious alternative to sugary drinks.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central) contains a direct entry for jasmine green tea with organic chia seed as a composite beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for brewed green tea and chia seeds at a typical café dilution ratio. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for green tea and chia seeds. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for brewed green tea (#14355) and chia seeds (#12006), weighted for a typical café preparation of ~250 ml brewed tea plus ~10 g dry chia seeds per 300 ml serving, then normalised to per-100 g basis. 2. Energy_kcal, protein_g, fat_g, and carbohydrate_g are all non-zero due to the chia seed component; none are naturally zero for this food. 3. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; chia seeds contain no trans fats). Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero; this is a plant-based beverage). Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for brewed green tea and chia seeds). Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; plant-based food). Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero; plant-based beverage with no UV exposure). 4. Fields set to -1 indicate values not reported in the source database: vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in USDA SR Legacy for this composite; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database; selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database. 5. Glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains negligible glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for essentially zero-GI beverages. GL per 300 g serving calculated as (1 × 5.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.1. 6. Cuisine tag in input was listed as 'Italian' which is inconsistent with the food; corrected to 'Singaporean Café' based on the restaurant context (The Light Cafe, Singapore) and the food's regional identity. 7. The input food_name included Chinese characters '茉莉花绿茶'; these have been preserved in local_names and aliases. 8. Multilingual names provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements. 9. All entries in aliases_by_language have been verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases list, including the mixed-script Chinese variant '茉莉花綠茶加有机奇亚籽' and the romanized forms 'Moli Hua Lü Cha' and 'Moli Hua Lü Cha Jia Youji Qiya Zi'.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected cuisine from erroneous 'Italian' to 'Singaporean Café' based on restaurant context and food identity.
Constructed composite nutrient estimates from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for brewed green tea and chia seeds.
Set glycemic_index to 1 per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule; gi_category set to 'low'.
Documented all -1 sentinel values with explicit 'not reported in the source database' justifications in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values (trans_fat, cholesterol, vitamin_c, vitamin_b12, vitamin_d) with 'naturally zero' or 'confirmed zero' justifications in notes.
Added full multilingual local_names and aliases for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements.

Performed exhaustive aliases consistency audit: collected all string values from every key in aliases_by_language and verified each appears verbatim in the flat aliases array. Added missing romanized entry 'Moli Hua Lü Cha Jia Youji Qiya Zi' and mixed-script Chinese entry '茉莉花綠茶加有机奇亚籽' to flat aliases to resolve q8 failure from iteration 2.
Selected defensible ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2, SNOMED CT) with closest_parent match type and gap notes.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and nutritional context.
